edge: VAR is a new generation paradigm that redefines autoregressive learning on images, focusing on coarse-to-fine 'next-scale prediction' or 'next-resolution prediction'.
VAR makes GPT-style AR models surpass diffusion transformers in image generation.
VAR outperforms the Diffusion Transformer (DiT) in multiple dimensions including image quality, inference speed, data efficiency, and scalability.

VAR showcases zero-shot generalization ability in downstream tasks including image in-painting, out-painting, and editing.

Scaling laws are found and studied in autoregressive language models, which describe a power-law relationship between the scale of model (or dataset, computation, etc.) and the cross-entropy loss value on the test set.
Scaling laws allow us to directly predict the performance of a larger model from smaller ones, thus guiding better resource allocation.
they show that the performance of LLMs can scale well with the growth of model, data, and computation and never saturate
The success brought by scaling laws has inspired the vision community to explore more similar methods for multimodality understanding and generation

VAR involves two separated training stages: Stage 1 and Stage 2.
A multi-scale VQ autoencoder encodes an image into K token maps R = (r1, r2, . . . , rK ) and is trained by a compound loss (5).
A VAR transformer is trained via next-scale prediction: it takes ([s], r1, r2, . . . , rK−1) as input to predict (r1, r2, r3, . . . , rK ).
The attention mask is used in training to ensure each rk can only attend to r≤k, which is a key aspect of the next-scale prediction method.
Standard cross-entropy loss is used in the next-scale prediction method, indicating a relationship between these two concepts.

The effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed VAR framework are verified in the ablation study.
The methodology of the vanilla AR transformer baseline implemented in [17] is replaced with VAR in the ablation study.
The first two rows of Table 3 compare GPT-2-style transformers trained under AR or VAR algorithm without any enhancements.
Subsequent lines in Table 3 show the influence of VAR enhancements such as 'AdaLN', 'CFG', 'Attn. Norm.', 'Cost', and '∆' relative to the baseline.
VAR achieves a way more better FID (18.65 vs. 5.22) with only 0.013× inference wall-clock cost than the AR model, demonstrating a leap in visual AR model’s performance and efficiency.
By replacing the standard Layer Normalization (LN) with Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN), VAR starts yielding better FID than the baseline.
By using the top-k sampling similar to the baseline, VAR’s FID is further improved compared to the baseline.

By using the classifier-free guidance (CFG) with ratio 1.5 and normalizing q and k to unit vectors before attention, we reach the FID of 3.30, which is 15.35 lower to the baseline, and its inference speed is still 45 times faster.
We finally scale up VAR size to 2.0B and achieve an FID of 1.73, which is 16.85 better than the baseline FID.
We mainly focus on the design of learning paradigm and keep the VQVAE architecture and training unchanged from the baseline to better justify VAR framework’s effectiveness.

The token dependency in VQVAE is examined through the attention scores in the self-attention mechanism, highlighting the significance of token dependency in VQVAE

The self-attention layer in [30] only has 1 head so for each image we just plot one attention map. The heat map in Fig. 9 shows the attention scores of each token to all other tokens, which indicate a strong, bidirectional dependency among all tokens. This is not surprising since the VQVAE model, trained to reconstruct images, leverages self-attention layers without any attention mask.
